docs.title: AI Safe Charity Index
docs.text: Practical AI safety for UK charities

docs.title: AI Safe Charity Index
docs.text:
An open-source, research-led knowledge base for the safe, responsible and practical use of AI in charities, from everyday prompts to tool approvals, risk controls and trustee oversight.

Answer the Quick Check I Already Made a Mistake Browse Task Guides Contact / Collaborate

docs.title: Quick CheckAnswer each question to get a route
docs.text:
1. Is this exact tool, account and feature approved for charity work? Yes, for this task Not sure No 2. What will you enter, upload, record or connect? Public, fictional or synthetic information Internal but not personal information Personal data about donors, staff, volunteers or service users Health, disability, HR, criminal-offence or other sensitive data Safeguarding, casework, children or adults at risk Passwords, API keys or security-sensitive information 3. What will the AI do? Draft, rewrite or summarise Search connected files, email, CRM or case systems Score, rank, profile or recommend something about people Send, publish, delete, spend money or change records 4. Could the output affect services, money, jobs, safeguarding or a public claim? No Maybe Yes 5. Will a named person check the output before it is used? Yes Not sure No Clear answers

Not enough information yet

The check will show a Green, Amber or Red route and link to the next page.

docs.text:
I know the task Browse Task Guides Find guidance for fundraising, meetings, services, HR, safeguarding, finance, research and communications.

I approve tools Approve a Use Case Use tool approval, account controls and DPIA triage before rollout.

Something went wrong Follow the Incident Route Stop the use, preserve facts and choose the data, safeguarding, cyber or content route.

Latest research Read the 2026 Evidence Snapshot Twenty findings from the July research packs on adoption, governance, trust, incidents and tool controls.

Help improve it Contact or Collaborate Suggest a correction, share a reliable source, request collaborator access or discuss research on AI policy in charities.

Already pasted, uploaded or connected something risky? Stop using the tool for that task and follow the AI incident route. Do not paste more information into the same system to investigate.

docs.title: Help Improve the Knowledge Base
docs.text:
This is an open-source, research-led resource. It gets better when charity staff, trustees, researchers and practitioners help test the guidance against real charity workflows.

Useful contributions include corrections, reliable sources, practical examples, accessibility feedback, plain-English review, template improvements and research on AI policy in charities.

Contact Matthew Request Collaborator Access Research and Community

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
The site is written primarily for charities in England and Wales. Much of the data-protection and cyber-security guidance is UK-wide, but charity law and safeguarding routes may differ by nation and organisation.

This guidance supports judgement. It does not replace legal advice, data-protection advice, safeguarding procedures, employment advice, procurement review or trustee decision-making.

docs.title: Current Status
docs.text: This site is in active development. Pages marked as draft or interim should be reviewed before being treated as final guidance.

docs.title: Templates
docs.text:
These templates are practical starting points. They do not replace legal, safeguarding, data-protection, employment or procurement advice.

Open the AI Governance Template Pack

docs.title: Operational Templates
docs.text: Template Use it when Owner One-page AI use proposal Someone wants to start or change an AI use Tool owner or manager Tool and feature approval record A charity approves a tool, plan, model, connector or agent Digital, procurement or data owner AI-use register The charity needs visibility of approved and pilot uses AI governance owner DPIA screening form A use may involve personal data or high-risk processing Data-protection lead Meeting transcription assessment A meeting may be recorded, transcribed or summarised by AI Meeting owner Human-review record AI output affects a person, public claim or important decision Accountable reviewer AI incident log Something may have gone wrong Incident owner Supplier questionnaire A vendor, agency or platform will provide AI capability Procurement or tool owner Trustee assurance dashboard Trustees need proportionate oversight Senior owner Content verification checklist AI output will be published or used externally Editor or communications lead

docs.title: Minimum Fields for an AI-Use Register
docs.text:
• use ID
  • owner
  • purpose
  • tool approval reference
  • data ceiling
  • people affected
  • human control
  • DPIA, safeguarding, equality and security screens
  • decision and conditions
  • review trigger and date

docs.title: Template Rule
docs.text: Keep templates short for small charities, but do not leave out ownership, data category, human review, incident route or review date.

docs.title: One-Page AI Use Proposal
docs.text:
• purpose and expected benefit
  • tool, plan, feature and model
  • data categories and data ceiling
  • people affected
  • human review
  • DPIA, safeguarding, equality and security screens
  • supplier or connector checks
  • incident route
  • owner, decision and review date

docs.title: Tool and Feature Approval Record
docs.text:
• exact product, account plan, workspace and feature
  • permitted users and permitted tasks
  • prohibited data and prohibited tasks
  • contract, DPA and official documentation links
  • training, retention, deletion and connector settings
  • evidence of admin settings
  • change triggers
  • retirement and offboarding route

docs.title: Meeting Transcription Assessment
docs.text:
• purpose and necessity
  • lawful basis and likely sensitive topics
  • participants and power imbalance
  • notice, objection and alternative route
  • exact tool and data flow
  • access, sharing, retention and deletion
  • accuracy owner
  • relationship between transcript, summary and official minutes
  • incident route

docs.title: AI Incident Log
docs.text:
• time detected and awareness time
  • reporter and incident owner
  • system, account, feature and connector
  • data and people affected
  • containment steps
  • evidence preserved
  • provider contact
  • privacy, safeguarding, cyber, fraud and content assessments
  • notifications and deadlines
  • decision reasons
  • remediation and closure

docs.title: Supplier Questionnaire
docs.text:
• controller, processor and subprocessor roles
  • processing purposes
  • training and product-improvement terms
  • retention, deletion and backups
  • human access by supplier staff
  • security, logging and audit controls
  • incident notice
  • international transfers
  • individual-rights support
  • connector and agent permissions
  • change notice, export and exit

docs.title: About
docs.text:
AI Safe Charity Index is an open, living resource for charities that want to use artificial intelligence responsibly.

It translates complex guidance into practical steps that charity staff, volunteers, managers and trustees can understand and use.

The project is guided by evidence, plain English, charity relevance, accessibility and human accountability.

docs.title: Creator
docs.text:
AI Safe Charity Index was originally created by Matthew Nel.

Connect with Matthew on LinkedIn

docs.title: Open Collaboration
docs.text:
The project invites constructive edits from collaborators who can improve the guidance, sources, accessibility, plain-English content, templates or charity relevance.

Researchers, practitioners, trustees, charity staff, data-protection professionals and safeguarding specialists are also invited to contribute evidence and research on AI policy in charities.

See the Contact page for collaboration and research topics.

docs.title: Matthew Nel
docs.text:
AI Safe Charity Index was originally created by Matthew Nel.

The project is being developed as an open, research-led resource for UK charities, and constructive edits, corrections and research contributions are welcome.

Connect on LinkedIn Contribute Research

docs.title: Collaborate on the Knowledge Base
docs.text:
You can help improve the project by:

  • suggesting edits to public guidance
  • flagging unclear or outdated content
  • sharing reliable sources
  • reviewing pages for charity relevance
  • reviewing pages for accessibility and plain English
  • suggesting practical examples for small and medium charities
  • improving templates, checklists and governance tools

Please avoid sharing personal data, safeguarding information, confidential charity material, passwords, API keys or private case details.

docs.title: Request Collaborator Access
docs.text:
People who want to contribute regularly can ask Matthew to add them as GitHub collaborators by invite. Use LinkedIn for the first request so Matthew can screen collaborator requests before sharing any direct email route.

To request collaborator access, contact Matthew and include:

  • your GitHub username
  • your name and organisation, if relevant
  • the type of contribution you want to make
  • the pages or topics you are interested in
  • any relevant review expertise, such as charity governance, AI policy, safeguarding, GDPR, fundraising, cyber security, accessibility, UX or plain English
  • whether you want to contribute research, edit content, review sources, improve templates or help with site design

Suggested subject line:

AI Safe Charity Index collaborator invite request

Request via LinkedIn

Do not include personal data, safeguarding case details, confidential charity information or private research responses in the initial message. If a conversation needs to move to email, Matthew can share an email address privately after the first contact.

docs.title: How GitHub Invites Work
docs.text:
Matthew, as project owner, can invite collaborators from the GitHub repository settings. GitHub sends the invitation to the person's GitHub account or email address, and the person must accept it before they can collaborate directly.

New collaborators should normally:

  • discuss the intended contribution first
  • work in branches or pull requests where practical
  • cite reliable sources for substantive claims
  • avoid publishing personal, safeguarding or confidential charity information
  • keep high-risk legal, data-protection, safeguarding and cyber-security content marked for review

docs.title: Research Collaboration
docs.text:
Matthew welcomes conversations with people interested in research on:

  • AI policy in charities
  • responsible AI governance for small and medium charities
  • trustee oversight of AI
  • charity staff confidence and training needs
  • AI, safeguarding and service delivery
  • AI and data protection in practical charity workflows
  • tool adoption, risk assessment and approved-tools processes

The project is especially interested in research that can help charities make safer, clearer and more proportionate decisions about AI.

docs.title: Corrections and Feedback
docs.text:
If you spot an error or want to suggest a source, please include:

  • the page title or URL
  • what should change
  • the source or evidence supporting the change
  • whether the issue is urgent or high risk

Do not use GitHub issues or public project spaces to share personal data, safeguarding information, confidential charity material, API keys or passwords.

docs.title: Can I paste personal data into an approved business AI account?
docs.text: Not automatically. Approval of the tool is only one condition. The specific use must also be necessary, lawful, minimised, transparent, secure and within the charity's policy and contract.

docs.title: Is a paid personal account safe for charity work?
docs.text: Payment alone does not create business terms, a Data Processing Agreement or admin controls. Use the charity's approved organisational account.

docs.title: Does removing a name make information anonymous?
docs.text: Not necessarily. A unique story or combination of details may still identify someone, and pseudonymised data can remain personal data.

docs.title: Can AI take meeting notes?
docs.text: Sometimes, but recording and transcription change the data-processing risk. Give appropriate notice, determine the lawful and fair basis, restrict access and retention, and avoid sensitive meetings unless specifically approved.

docs.title: Does everyone have to consent to AI meeting notes?
docs.text: Not always. Consent is context-specific and may not be the right lawful basis where people do not have a genuine choice. The charity still needs a lawful basis, clear notice, access and retention controls, and a route for objections or alternatives where appropriate.

docs.title: Can trustees use AI to make decisions?
docs.text: AI can assist, but trustees remain responsible and should not rely on AI output alone for material decisions.

docs.title: Are AI citations reliable?
docs.text: No citation should be trusted without opening the source and confirming that it supports the exact claim.

docs.title: What if a connector can see all my files?
docs.text: Stop and ask for an approval check. A connector can expose whatever your existing permissions expose, including files that were overshared before AI was enabled.

docs.title: When do we need a DPIA?
docs.text: Screen for a DPIA before AI use that may create high risk, especially where it involves vulnerable people, special category data, monitoring, profiling, decisions about people, large-scale data or broad connectors.

docs.title: Is "not used for training" enough?
docs.text: No. It is useful, but you still need to check retention, deletion, subprocessors, transfers, human access, security, connectors, output risk and your own account settings.

docs.title: Can volunteers use their own AI accounts?
docs.text: Only for charity work if the charity has explicitly approved that route. Personal accounts should not receive personal, confidential, safeguarding or internal charity information.

docs.title: Can we use AI-generated images of beneficiaries?
docs.text: Only with care. Do not present fictional people, quotes or stories as real. Synthetic or composite content should be lawful, ethical, clearly disclosed where needed and consistent with safeguarding and communications standards.

docs.title: Agentic AI
docs.text: AI that can plan steps and act through tools or connected systems, not only produce an answer.

docs.title: Anonymisation
docs.text: Changing information so a person is no longer identifiable by anyone reasonably likely to access it. This is stronger than simply removing a name.

docs.title: Article 6 Lawful Basis
docs.text: The UK GDPR basis that allows personal data to be processed for a specific purpose.

docs.title: Article 9 Condition
docs.text: An additional condition needed for processing special category data, alongside an Article 6 lawful basis.

docs.title: Automated Decision
docs.text: A decision produced by automated processing. Legal rules become especially important where there is no meaningful human involvement and the effect is significant.

docs.title: Connector
docs.text: A link that lets an AI retrieve information from or act in another service, such as email, file storage, CRM or project-management software.

docs.title: Controller
docs.text: The organisation that decides why and how personal data is processed.

docs.title: Criminal-Offence Data
docs.text: Personal data relating to criminal convictions, offences, allegations or related security measures.

docs.title: Data Flow
docs.text: A map of where data comes from, which tool or supplier receives it, where it is stored, who can access it and where it goes next.

docs.title: Data Minimisation
docs.text: Using only the personal data genuinely needed for the purpose.

docs.title: Data Subject
docs.text: The identified or identifiable person the personal data is about.

docs.title: DPIA
docs.text: A Data Protection Impact Assessment. A structured assessment required before processing likely to create high risk to people.

docs.title: Fine-Tuning
docs.text: Training a model further on selected data to change how it behaves for a specific purpose.

docs.title: Foundation Model
docs.text: A general model trained on broad data that can be adapted or prompted for many tasks.

docs.title: Grounding
docs.text: Giving an AI system specific sources or data to use when answering.

docs.title: Hallucination
docs.text: An AI output that sounds plausible but is wrong, unsupported or invented.

docs.title: Human Oversight
docs.text: Competent human involvement that can identify problems and change the result.

docs.title: Inference
docs.text: Running data through a trained model to produce an output.

docs.title: International Transfer
docs.text: Moving or making personal data accessible outside the UK in a way covered by transfer rules.

docs.title: Legal or Similarly Significant Effect
docs.text: An effect that changes someone's legal rights or has similarly serious practical impact, such as access to work, support, credit, services or important opportunities.

docs.title: Model
docs.text: The AI system that produces predictions, summaries, classifications, images, text or other outputs.

docs.title: Personal-Data Breach
docs.text: A security incident that leads to accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, disclosure of, or access to personal data.

docs.title: Plugin or App
docs.text: An added capability that lets an AI tool connect to another system, retrieve data or perform actions.

docs.title: Processor
docs.text: An organisation that processes personal data on a controller's instructions.

docs.title: Profiling
docs.text: Automated processing that evaluates or predicts aspects of a person, such as performance, interests, behaviour, risk, eligibility or preferences.

docs.title: Prompt Injection
docs.text: Instructions hidden in content that try to make an AI system reveal information or act unexpectedly.

docs.title: Pseudonymisation
docs.text: Replacing identifiers while keeping separate information that could reconnect the data to a person. Pseudonymised data can remain personal data.

docs.title: Red Teaming
docs.text: Testing a system by deliberately trying to make it fail, reveal information or behave unsafely.

docs.title: Retention
docs.text: How long information is kept before deletion, archiving or review.

docs.title: Solely Automated Decision
docs.text: A decision made without meaningful human involvement.

docs.title: Special Category Data
docs.text: Sensitive personal data such as health, ethnicity, religion, political opinions, trade-union membership, sex life, sexual orientation, genetic or biometric data used for identification.

docs.title: Subprocessor
docs.text: A further supplier used by a processor to help process personal data.

docs.title: System Prompt
docs.text: Instructions set by the tool or organisation that guide how the AI should behave.

docs.title: Synthetic Media
docs.text: AI-generated or altered image, audio or video.

docs.title: Training
docs.text: Using data to build or improve a model's behaviour.

docs.title: Accessibility
docs.text:
The project aims to meet WCAG 2.2 AA wherever practical.

Accessibility is part of editorial quality, technical quality and public trust. Guidance should use clear headings, descriptive links, plain English, accessible tables, meaningful alt text and text labels that do not rely on colour alone.

Green, Amber and Red guidance must always include text labels and explanations, not colour alone.

docs.title: What We Aim To Do
docs.text:
• use clear headings and landmarks
  • keep link text descriptive
  • support keyboard navigation
  • preserve visible focus states
  • avoid colour-only meaning
  • write in plain English
  • keep tables usable on small screens
  • provide text alternatives for meaningful images
  • use captions or transcripts where media is added
  • test important pages manually as well as with automated checks

docs.title: AI-Generated Content
docs.text:
AI-generated or AI-assisted content must be checked by a person before publication for:

  • headings and structure
  • factual accuracy
  • plain-English clarity
  • alt text
  • captions and transcripts
  • colour contrast
  • bias, stereotypes and exclusion
  • whether synthetic content could mislead people

Automated captions, translations, summaries and alt text should be treated as drafts.

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text: The site is still in early development. Accessibility review should be repeated after major navigation, template, form or media changes.

docs.title: AI and Data Protection
docs.text:
This section explains the data-protection concepts charities need when using AI tools.

UK data-protection law is technology-neutral. If an AI use processes personal data, the charity still needs to meet the usual principles: lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, data minimisation, accuracy, storage limitation, security and accountability.

docs.title: July 2026 Position
docs.text:
The Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 amended the UK GDPR, Data Protection Act 2018 and PECR. The main DUAA data-protection and privacy changes relevant to most charities are now in force, but commencement was staged and current ICO guidance should be checked for the provision in question.

The DUAA does not make AI automatically safe or compliant. It changes some rules and creates some opportunities, but charities still need a lawful basis, fairness, transparency, security, minimisation and safeguards.

docs.title: The Lifecycle to Check
docs.text:
Before using AI with personal data, check the full lifecycle:

  • the purpose of the use, before choosing the tool
  • what personal data is involved
  • whether special category or criminal-offence data is involved
  • whether children or adults at risk could be affected
  • whether the charity is controller, joint controller or processor
  • whether the provider is a processor, controller or another role
  • whether the use is necessary and proportionate
  • whether the tool and account are approved
  • whether a Data Processing Agreement or other contract is needed
  • whether the use is likely to need a DPIA
  • whether people need to be told about the use
  • whether the output could affect someone significantly
  • how records, prompts, outputs, logs and recordings will be retained or deleted
  • what incident route applies if something goes wrong

A purpose is not just 'using AI'

A purpose is something specific, such as drafting public event copy, transcribing an advisory-group meeting, or summarising survey themes. Development, testing and live deployment may need separate checks.

docs.title: Personal Data and Pseudonymisation
docs.text:
Removing a name does not automatically make information anonymous. A distinctive story, rare role, location, condition, incident or combination of details can still identify someone.

Pseudonymised information can remain personal data where it can be reconnected to a person.

docs.title: Automated Decisions and Profiling
docs.text:
Interim guidance

The ICO says updated automated decision-making and profiling guidance is in drafting, with final guidance due in winter 2026. Treat this page as interim and seek data-protection or legal review before using AI for significant decisions about people.

AI can support decisions, but it must not quietly become the decision-maker for important outcomes.

The DUAA changed the UK framework for automated decision-making. This page separates legal Article 22-style questions from the wider safety, fairness and accountability questions that still apply even where Article 22 is not engaged.

docs.title: Assistive Use
docs.text: AI helps a person draft, summarise or organise information. A competent person makes the final judgement.

docs.title: Recommendation or Scoring
docs.text: AI ranks, scores, predicts or recommends something about a person. This is higher risk and may need a DPIA.

docs.title: Significant Automated Decision
docs.text: AI or automated processing makes a decision with legal or similarly significant effects without meaningful human involvement. This is Red until reviewed.

docs.title: Examples That Need Care
docs.text:
• recruitment scoring or filtering
  • service eligibility decisions
  • grant or support prioritisation
  • disciplinary or grievance decisions
  • risk scoring for beneficiaries
  • donor profiling that affects treatment or contact
  • decisions involving children or adults at risk

docs.title: Meaningful Human Involvement
docs.text: Human involvement should be real. The person reviewing the output should have enough information, skill and authority to challenge or change the result.

docs.title: Safeguards to Consider
docs.text:
• DPIA before deployment
  • clear explanation to affected people
  • human intervention route
  • challenge or appeal route
  • bias and accuracy testing
  • monitoring after launch
  • clear record of responsibility
  • special review where special category data is involved

docs.title: Six-Question Route
docs.text:
Ask these questions before using AI in any decision about a person:

  1. Is there personal data?
  2. Is there special category or criminal-offence data?
  3. Does the processing involve profiling, scoring, ranking, prediction or recommendation?
  4. Could the outcome have a legal or similarly significant effect?
  5. Is there meaningful human involvement before the decision takes effect?
  6. Even if the strict automated-decision rule is not triggered, is the use still lawful, fair, accurate, transparent, secure and non-discriminatory?

docs.title: Example Decisions to Treat as Red
docs.text:
• rejecting, deprioritising or scoring a service user for support
  • filtering job applicants
  • ranking grant applicants
  • flagging someone for fraud, risk or safeguarding action
  • disciplinary, grievance or performance-management recommendations
  • donor profiles that materially change treatment or contact

docs.title: Review Trigger
docs.text: Review this page after the ICO publishes final updated automated-decision guidance following the DUAA changes.

docs.text:

  • GOV.UK: Data Use and Access Act 2025 data protection and privacy changes

docs.title: DPIA Triage for AI
docs.text:
A Data Protection Impact Assessment is required before processing that is likely to result in a high risk to people's rights and freedoms.

This page is a triage aid, not a completed DPIA.

docs.title: Start With These Questions
docs.text:
Ask whether the AI use involves:

  • personal data
  • special category data
  • criminal-offence data
  • children or adults at risk
  • donor, beneficiary, staff or volunteer records
  • profiling, scoring or ranking people
  • automated recommendations about people
  • monitoring staff, volunteers, donors or service users
  • large-scale processing
  • new technology in a way people may not expect
  • connected systems such as email, file stores, CRM, HR or case-management tools

If several apply, treat the use as likely to need a DPIA or specialist review.

docs.title: Strong DPIA Triggers
docs.text:
Complete a DPIA before proceeding if the AI use could:

  • affect access to support, grants, employment or services
  • assess risk, vulnerability, eligibility or priority
  • process safeguarding, health, disability or other special category information
  • use personal data in a new or unexpected way
  • connect to a large body of internal files or records
  • create a high risk if the output is wrong, biased or leaked

docs.title: What the DPIA Should Cover
docs.text:
For AI, include:

  • purpose and necessity
  • data categories and sources
  • lawful basis and special-category condition where relevant
  • tool, plan and supplier role
  • retention and deletion
  • training or model-improvement settings
  • connectors and third-party actions
  • international transfers
  • security controls
  • human review
  • bias and accuracy testing
  • incident route
  • review date

docs.title: Record the Screening Decision
docs.text:
Even where the answer is "no DPIA needed", record:

  • date of screening
  • owner and reviewer
  • purpose
  • data categories
  • people affected
  • whether children, adults at risk or vulnerable people may be affected
  • whether the use involves monitoring, profiling, matching, invisible processing, denial of service, connectors or automated actions
  • outcome and reasons
  • next review trigger

docs.title: Consult People Where Appropriate
docs.text: For higher-risk AI use, consider whether affected people, staff, volunteers, service leads, safeguarding leads or lived-experience groups should be consulted before launch. Consultation should be proportionate and safe.

docs.title: Residual Risk and ICO Consultation
docs.text: The DPIA should record whether risks remain after controls are chosen. If high residual risk cannot be reduced, seek specialist advice. The ICO expects consultation before starting processing where high risk remains.

docs.title: Review Triggers
docs.text:
Re-screen when there is a material change, including:

  • new model
  • new data source
  • new connector or agent action
  • new purpose
  • new audience or affected group
  • new output used in decisions
  • provider term change
  • incident or near miss
  • material accuracy, bias or security concern

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• ICO: When do we need to do a DPIA?
  • ICO: How do we do a DPIA?
  • ICO: Guidance on AI and data protection

docs.title: Individual Rights
docs.text: AI use does not remove people's data-protection rights. In some cases, it makes rights harder to handle unless the charity keeps good records.

docs.title: Rights to Plan For
docs.text:
Depending on the use, people may need routes to:

  • be told how their data is used
  • access personal data held about them
  • correct inaccurate data
  • object to certain processing
  • ask for restriction or erasure where the legal conditions apply
  • challenge a significant automated decision
  • make a complaint

docs.title: Records That Make Rights Work
docs.text:
Keep enough information to answer:

  • which tool, account and feature was used
  • what personal data was entered, uploaded, connected or generated
  • whether the provider stores prompts, outputs, files, logs or recordings
  • who can retrieve or delete the data
  • whether outputs influenced a decision about a person
  • what human review happened

docs.title: AI Output Can Be Personal Data
docs.text:
An AI-generated summary, score, profile, prediction or note about an identifiable person may itself be personal data. It may also be inaccurate, incomplete or unfair.

Do not copy AI output into a case record, HR file, donor record or safeguarding record unless a competent person has checked it and the record clearly fits the charity's purpose.

docs.title: When to Escalate
docs.text:
Escalate to the data-protection lead if:

  • a person asks whether AI was used about them
  • an AI output may be wrong in a record
  • an AI system produced a score, risk rating or recommendation
  • a supplier cannot support access, correction or deletion
  • the use involves automated decision-making or profiling

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• ICO: Individual rights
  • ICO: How do we ensure transparency in AI?
  • ICO: Rights related to automated decision-making including profiling

docs.title: Lawful Basis and Transparency
docs.text:
Define the purpose before choosing the tool or lawful basis. "Using AI" is not a purpose.

A useful purpose sounds more like:

  • drafting a first version of a public event description
  • transcribing a meeting so an authorised employee can prepare agreed minutes
  • summarising survey themes after direct identifiers have been removed
  • helping a trained caseworker find public guidance faster

If personal data is involved, record the Article 6 lawful basis before processing starts. If special category or criminal-offence data is involved, identify the additional legal condition and safeguards.

docs.title: Consent Needs Real Choice
docs.text:
Consent is appropriate only where people can refuse or withdraw without detriment. Be especially cautious where the person is an employee, volunteer, service user, child, adult at risk or someone who depends on the charity's support.

For many charity settings, a clear explanation, a lawful basis other than consent, and a workable alternative may be more realistic than asking for consent that is not freely given.

docs.title: What to Tell People
docs.text:
People should be able to understand:

  • that AI is being used where this materially affects the processing
  • what personal data is used
  • why it is used
  • who receives it
  • whether a supplier processes it
  • whether it may be transferred internationally
  • how long it is kept
  • how human review works
  • how to exercise rights or complain

docs.title: Small-Charity Check
docs.text:
Before the use starts, write down:

Question Minimum record What is the purpose? One plain-English sentence What data is used? Categories, not a vague label What lawful basis applies? Article 6 basis and reason Is special category data involved? Article 9 condition or "not involved" How are people told? Privacy notice, meeting notice, script or other route What is the human role? Named reviewer or role

docs.text:

  • ICO: How do we ensure transparency in AI?
  • ICO: When is consent appropriate?
  • ICO: Create your own privacy notice

docs.title: Retention, Security and Breaches
docs.text: AI tools can create extra copies of information: prompts, uploaded files, outputs, embeddings, transcripts, logs, audit records, feedback data and support tickets.

docs.title: Retention Questions
docs.text:
For each approved use, record:

  • what the charity keeps
  • what the provider keeps
  • how long prompts, files, recordings and outputs remain available
  • whether deletion affects backups or logs
  • who can delete or export data
  • what happens when a user leaves
  • what happens when the charity stops using the tool

docs.title: Security Questions
docs.text:
Check:

  • MFA, SSO and role-based access
  • admin visibility and audit logs
  • connector permissions
  • file-sharing permissions before enabling AI search
  • restrictions for secrets, credentials and sensitive folders
  • provider security documentation
  • incident notice and escalation
  • emergency disable or rollback process

docs.title: Mistaken Disclosure
docs.text:
If personal, confidential, safeguarding or security-sensitive information is pasted, uploaded or exposed:

  1. Stop using the conversation, file or agent for that task.
  2. Do not hide the mistake or destroy evidence.
  3. Tell the incident contact immediately.
  4. Record the exact tool, account, feature, date, prompt, upload, connector and people affected.
  5. Preserve output and audit evidence according to the incident lead's instructions.
  6. Follow the AI incident route.

docs.title: 72-Hour Assessment
docs.text: Not every AI incident is a reportable personal-data breach. Where a personal-data breach is likely to result in a risk to people's rights and freedoms, the charity should normally notify the ICO within 72 hours of becoming aware of it. Record the assessment and reasons either way.

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• ICO: Personal data breaches
  • ICO: 72 hours - how to respond to a personal data breach
  • ICO AI audit framework: Security and integrity
  • NCSC: AI and cyber security

docs.title: Special Category, Children and Vulnerability
docs.text: General-purpose AI tools should not be used with sensitive information unless the use has been specifically approved, contracted and assessed.

docs.title: Red Until Reviewed
docs.text:
Treat these as Red until a specialist route approves them:

  • safeguarding concerns or case notes
  • health, disability, mental-health or care information
  • ethnicity, religion, sexual orientation or trade-union information
  • criminal-offence or allegation information
  • information about children or adults at risk
  • domestic abuse, homelessness, debt, migration, addiction or crisis-service records
  • beneficiary stories where a person could be recognised from context

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text: Sensitive data can cause serious harm if exposed, misread or used unfairly. AI can also infer sensitive characteristics from ordinary details, such as location, service use, language, symptoms, or lived-experience descriptions.

docs.title: Minimum Controls Before Any Approved Use
docs.text:
• confirm the purpose is necessary and proportionate
  • identify the Article 6 lawful basis
  • identify the Article 9 condition, or other legal condition for criminal-offence data
  • complete DPIA screening, and a full DPIA where high risk is likely
  • involve safeguarding, service or clinical leads where relevant
  • use a contracted organisational tool, not a personal account
  • limit prompts, files, connectors, outputs and retention
  • define human review and escalation
  • record what happens if the AI output is wrong or harmful

docs.title: Safer Alternatives
docs.text:
• use fictional or synthetic examples
  • replace case notes with high-level issue summaries
  • ask for a structure, checklist or policy outline without real details
  • use approved internal case-management, safeguarding or clinical systems
  • involve a trained person directly rather than using AI

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• ICO: Special category data
  • ICO: Children and the UK GDPR
  • Charity Commission: Safeguarding and protecting people for charities and trustees

docs.title: Suppliers, Transfers and Contracts
docs.text: Do not approve an AI supplier from a product page alone. Approve the role, contract, data flow, controls and exact feature.

docs.title: Controller or Processor?
docs.text:
Before using an AI tool with personal data, decide whether the supplier is:

  • a processor acting only on the charity's instructions
  • a separate controller for some purposes
  • a joint controller
  • a subprocessor or model provider behind another service

This affects the contract, transparency wording, rights handling and accountability.

docs.title: Contract Checks
docs.text:
Check for:

  • processing purposes and instructions
  • training or product-improvement use
  • retention, deletion and backups
  • human access by supplier staff
  • subprocessors and model providers
  • international transfers and safeguards
  • security controls
  • incident notice
  • help with rights requests, DPIAs and audits
  • connector and agent permissions
  • data export and exit
  • notice of material changes

docs.title: Red Flags
docs.text:
• no clear organisation contract
  • only consumer terms for charity work
  • unclear training or retention terms
  • no subprocessor list
  • no deletion route
  • no incident notification commitment
  • broad connectors without admin controls
  • supplier can change model/provider materially without notice

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• ICO: How do you determine whether you are a controller or processor?
  • ICO AI audit framework: Contracts and third parties
  • ICO: Data sharing agreements

docs.title: Before You Paste
docs.text:
The core safety check

This framework does not replace professional advice, a Data Protection Impact Assessment, safeguarding procedures, supplier due diligence or formal organisational approval.

docs.title: Before You Paste
docs.text: Before you paste information into an AI tool, stop and check what the information contains, where it may go, what the tool may do with it, and what could happen if the output is wrong.

docs.title: The Five-Step Pause
docs.text:
Ask these questions in order:

1

2

3

4

5

A paid personal account is not a work-approved account

Do not assume that paying for an individual AI subscription gives the charity a contract, Data Processing Agreement, admin controls, audit logs, retention controls or suitable data protections.

docs.title: Approved Tool
docs.text: Is the tool approved for work? If not, stop and use an approved route or ask for approval.

docs.title: Information Type
docs.text: Check whether it is public, internal, confidential, personal, special category, safeguarding-related or security-sensitive.

docs.title: Necessity
docs.text: Remove, generalise or replace details wherever possible.

docs.title: AI Action
docs.text: Risk rises when AI analyses people, recommends decisions, contacts people, searches connected systems or takes actions.

docs.title: Human Check
docs.text: A named person must be able to understand, challenge and change the output.

docs.title: Green: Go Ahead With Normal Checks
docs.text:
Green uses are usually low risk. The information is public, fictional, synthetic or genuinely non-personal. The tool is approved for the task, no significant decision about a person is being made, and a person will review the output.

Examples:

  • brainstorming campaign names
  • rewriting already-public website copy
  • summarising a public report
  • creating a meeting agenda
  • generating fictional examples

Green does not mean the output is automatically accurate.

docs.title: Amber: Pause and Check
docs.text:
Amber means pause for the documented route before you continue. Depending on the task, that may mean a manager, data-protection lead, safeguarding lead, HR, fundraising, IT/security, procurement or a DPIA screen.

Pause when the task involves:

  • personal data
  • internal information
  • unpublished documents
  • donor, staff, volunteer or beneficiary information
  • decisions that could affect people
  • unclear tool settings or account terms
  • connected apps such as email, file storage, CRM, Slack, SharePoint, Google Drive or Notion
  • meeting transcription, donor segmentation, HR drafting or research that may influence policy

Check your charity's policy, speak to the right internal lead, remove unnecessary information, and use an approved route where one exists. A manager cannot waive data-protection, safeguarding, employment, fundraising or trustee duties.

docs.title: Red: Stop
docs.text:
Stop when the information includes:

  • safeguarding case details
  • passwords, API keys or security credentials
  • special category data without an approved arrangement
  • information about children or adults at risk where someone could be identified
  • confidential legal, HR or disciplinary material
  • identifiable beneficiary case notes
  • information for an automated decision that could significantly affect someone
  • autonomous agents that can send messages, publish, delete records, spend money or change systems without approval gates

Use your charity's established safeguarding, data-protection, legal, HR or senior-management process instead.

docs.title: What Meaningful Human Review Means
docs.text:
Human review is not just glancing at the answer.

The reviewer should:

  • understand the task and the risk
  • check facts against reliable sources
  • look for bias, missing context and unsafe wording
  • confirm the output follows charity policy
  • have authority to reject or change the result
  • record the review where the decision is important

docs.title: If You Paste Something by Mistake
docs.text:
If you accidentally enter personal, confidential, safeguarding or security-sensitive information into an AI tool:

  1. Stop using that chat or tool for the task.
  2. Tell your manager or the relevant data-protection, safeguarding or security lead.
  3. Record what was shared, which tool/account was used and when.
  4. Do not try to fix it by pasting more information.
  5. Follow the charity's incident process.

docs.title: Safer Alternatives
docs.text:
When in doubt:

  • use a fictional example
  • remove identifiers
  • summarise the issue at a higher level
  • use public information
  • ask for structure or wording help without sharing the real case
  • use an approved internal tool
  • ask a manager, safeguarding lead or data-protection lead

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• ICO: Guidance on AI and data protection
  • Charity Commission: Charities and Artificial Intelligence
  • Fundraising Regulator: Guidance for using artificial intelligence in fundraising
  • NCSC: AI and cyber security

docs.title: Everyday Charity Work
docs.text:
Find guidance by task

A communications task can become high risk if it uses a real beneficiary story. A finance task can be low risk if it uses dummy data and a human checks the result.

docs.title: Everyday Charity Work
docs.text: Use this section when you know the task you want to do. Risk follows the task, data and impact, not the department.

docs.title: Task Guides
docs.text:
Public content Communications Drafting, editing, synthetic media and public claims.

Donors and campaigns Fundraising Appeals, donor data, segmentation and fundraising ethics.

Recording and notes Meetings Transcription, summaries, notice, access and retention.

Service delivery Beneficiary Services Chatbots, advice boundaries and human escalation.

High-risk route Safeguarding Children, adults at risk, case notes and escalation.

People decisions Recruitment and HR Shortlisting, monitoring, employee data and fairness.

Volunteer management Volunteers Onboarding, rota support, safeguarding and personal accounts.

Money and controls Finance, Grants and Fraud Payments, bank details, grant ranking and fraud flags.

Evidence and learning Research and Evaluation Survey themes, interviews, anonymisation and policy evidence.

Inclusive access Translation and Inclusion Accessible drafts, translation checks and sensitive contexts.

Digital delivery Website, Code and Analytics Code help, analytics, cookies, security and public tools.

docs.title: Before You Start
docs.text:
Ask:

  • Is the exact tool, plan and feature approved?
  • What information will be entered, uploaded, recorded or connected?
  • Could the output affect a person?
  • Who checks the result?
  • What happens if the tool is wrong, biased or leaks information?

docs.title: AI in Fundraising
docs.text:
AI can help fundraisers draft, plan, test and summarise. It can also create risks around misleading content, donor privacy, vulnerability, profiling and trust.

The Fundraising Regulator says charitable fundraising institutions remain responsible and accountable for AI use in fundraising, including AI-generated outputs they use.

docs.title: July 2026 Evidence Update
docs.text:
The latest research packs reinforce three practical fundraising rules:

  • AI can be useful for drafts, structure, research and first-pass copy, but the charity remains accountable for accuracy, tone and truthfulness.
  • Donor segmentation, personalised asks, wealth screening, supporter profiling and automated stewardship changes should be treated as Amber or Red depending on data, transparency and human oversight.
  • Synthetic beneficiary stories or AI-generated images must not be presented as real experience. If synthetic or composite material is used at all, it needs clear labelling, senior review and a trust-risk check.

docs.title: Green Uses
docs.text:
Usually suitable with normal review:

  • brainstorming public campaign ideas
  • drafting generic social posts
  • improving the clarity of public copy
  • turning published impact information into a first-draft checklist
  • creating internal planning structures without donor data

docs.title: Amber Uses
docs.text:
Pause and check before using AI for:

  • donor segmentation
  • fundraising emails based on supporter data
  • grant applications using internal evidence
  • AI-generated images or stories for public campaigns
  • analysis of survey comments
  • personalised donor communications
  • fundraising content about vulnerable people

docs.title: Red Uses
docs.text:
Do not use a general-purpose or unapproved AI tool for:

  • identifiable donor databases
  • special category data
  • fabricated testimonials or impact evidence
  • synthetic beneficiary stories presented as real
  • decisions about who should receive support, grants or contact without safeguards
  • fundraising approaches that could exploit vulnerability

docs.title: Fundraising AI Checklist
docs.text:
Before using AI:

  • confirm the tool and account are approved
  • assess risk before the use starts
  • check whether donor or supporter data is involved
  • apply data minimisation
  • review for accuracy and fairness
  • check Code of Fundraising Practice expectations
  • decide whether transparency about AI use is needed
  • keep a record of important checks
  • involve trustees for strategic or high-risk use

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• Fundraising Regulator: Guidance for using artificial intelligence in fundraising
  • Fundraising Regulator: Data privacy and fundraising
  • Fundraising Regulator: Charitable purposes soft opt-in and fundraising marketing

docs.title: Beneficiary Services and Chatbots
docs.text: AI that interacts with beneficiaries is not comparable to AI that drafts internal copy. The consequences of wrong, harmful or inaccessible output can be serious.

docs.title: July 2026 Evidence Update
docs.text:
The latest research packs separate safer administrative support from high-risk beneficiary-facing use:

  • Safer uses are usually internal, anonymised and human-reviewed, such as grouping anonymised feedback or drafting service-information summaries from approved sources.
  • Higher-risk uses include public chatbots, helpline triage, personalised recommendations, translation of sensitive conversations and any tool connected to case records.
  • Red-line uses include crisis support, counselling, safeguarding advice, medical advice, legal advice, automated eligibility decisions or autonomous triage without trained human review.

docs.title: Four Levels of Use
docs.text: Level Example Default risk Navigation Points to opening times or human contact routes Amber Information support Summarises approved public information Amber Personalised recommendation Uses a person's circumstances to suggest options Red until reviewed Decision or professional advice Determines eligibility, risk, safeguarding or crisis action Red

docs.title: Mandatory Controls
docs.text:
Any public bot or advice tool should:

  • say clearly that it is AI
  • never imply a human is responding
  • show urgent-help and human-contact routes
  • use approved, maintained knowledge sources
  • avoid unnecessary personal data collection
  • have retention information
  • be tested for harmful advice and hallucination
  • hand off safeguarding disclosures to a trained route
  • be monitored for complaints and unequal outcomes
  • have a kill switch

docs.title: Red Lines
docs.text:
Do not use a general-purpose AI tool as:

  • a counsellor
  • a crisis service
  • a safeguarding lead
  • a medical professional
  • a legal adviser
  • a gatekeeper for support

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• Charity Commission: Safeguarding and protecting people
  • UK Government: Data and AI Ethics Framework
  • NHS England: Artificial intelligence information governance guidance

docs.title: Communications and Public Content
docs.text: AI can help draft, reformat and test public content, but a charity remains responsible for accuracy, dignity, accessibility and trust.

docs.title: Green Uses
docs.text:
• brainstorming campaign concepts from public information
  • turning approved source text into format variants
  • suggesting headings, alt-text drafts or plain-language alternatives
  • creating clearly fictional early-stage visual concepts

docs.title: Amber Uses
docs.text:
• public content about a real service, community or lived experience
  • synthetic images of people or places
  • fundraising stories that could be mistaken for real examples
  • translation, captions or accessibility summaries for public use
  • content using partner, funder or licensed assets

docs.title: Red Uses
docs.text:
• fabricated facts, quotations, case studies or research findings
  • synthetic beneficiary stories presented as real
  • AI-generated evidence about a real incident
  • impersonation of a real person
  • public legal, medical, safeguarding or crisis advice without qualified review

docs.title: Minimum Publication Check
docs.text:
Before publishing AI-assisted content:

  • verify every factual claim against the original source
  • confirm permission for real stories, images and quotes
  • check for stereotypes, bias and harmful framing
  • decide whether synthetic content needs disclosure
  • check accessibility in the final format
  • name the human editor responsible for the final version

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• ASA: Disclosure of AI in advertising
  • CAP Code: Misleading advertising
  • UK Government: Copyright and artificial intelligence report

docs.title: Finance, Grants and Fraud
docs.text: AI can support finance and grant work, but payment authority and financial controls must stay with accountable people.

docs.title: Lower-Risk Support
docs.text:
• explaining a public budget template
  • drafting a narrative from verified aggregate figures
  • suggesting spreadsheet formulas for testing on a copy
  • summarising published grant criteria

docs.title: High-Risk Uses
docs.text:
• uploading payroll, bank, donor or supplier records to an unapproved tool
  • changing payment details from AI-generated or email-only instructions
  • autonomous payments, refunds or purchases
  • eligibility or grant scoring without a governed process
  • accepting generated financial analysis without reconciliation
  • sharing live credentials or security configuration

docs.title: Controls
docs.text:
• reconcile outputs to source systems
  • preserve segregation of duties
  • independently verify bank-detail changes through a trusted channel
  • treat urgent, secret or unusual requests as potential fraud
  • keep payment authority outside the agent
  • log changes and retain source evidence
  • include finance and cyber owners in incident exercises

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• Charity Commission: Internal financial controls for charities
  • NCSC: Small organisations guide to cyber security
  • Charity Sector Risk Assessment 2025

docs.title: AI Meeting Notes and Transcription
docs.text:
AI meeting notes can save time, but recording and transcription can capture personal data, confidential discussions, special category data and third-party voices.

Do not treat transcription as a default admin feature.

Consent is context-specific

Consent is not always the lawful basis for recording or transcription. The charity must identify a lawful basis, and where special category data is likely an additional condition may be needed. Even where consent is not the lawful basis, participants should normally receive clear advance notice and a workable route to raise concerns.

docs.title: Before Recording or Transcribing
docs.text:
Check:

  • the purpose of the recording or transcript
  • who will be recorded
  • whether personal or sensitive topics may arise
  • whether everyone has been given clear notice
  • the lawful and fair basis for processing
  • who can access the transcript and summary
  • how long the recording and transcript will be kept
  • whether the tool/account is approved
  • whether the meeting is too sensitive for AI notes
  • whether participants may feel unable to object
  • how objections, alternatives or safeguarding exceptions will be handled

docs.title: Usually Lower Risk
docs.text:
• public webinar planning
  • routine project meetings with no personal data
  • agenda and action drafting after a human checks the result

docs.title: Higher Risk
docs.text:
• HR meetings
  • complaints
  • safeguarding discussions
  • beneficiary case meetings
  • trustee disputes
  • legal or contract negotiations
  • meetings involving children or vulnerable people

docs.title: Controls
docs.text:
• provide advance notice
  • explain the purpose and tool
  • restrict access to recordings and transcripts
  • avoid sensitive meetings unless specifically approved
  • set retention periods
  • check summaries for errors
  • delete recordings when no longer needed
  • record the decision for high-risk meetings
  • do not use a transcript as the official record until a responsible person has checked it

docs.title: Ask for Help When
docs.text:
• participants may feel unable to object
  • special category or safeguarding information may arise
  • the tool stores audio or transcript data outside your control
  • the transcript could affect an employment, safeguarding or service decision

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• ICO: Guidance on video surveillance, including audio and monitoring principles
  • DWP: Generic recording and transcription policy
  • ACAS: What happens after an investigation

docs.title: Recruitment and HR
docs.text: AI use in recruitment and HR can affect people's work, income, dignity and rights. Treat scoring, ranking, monitoring and disciplinary uses as high risk.

docs.title: Green Uses
docs.text:
• drafting a generic job description from approved role information
  • creating interview-question ideas for human review
  • producing a training outline
  • rewriting public HR guidance in plain English

docs.title: Amber Uses
docs.text:
• anonymised aggregate staff-survey summaries
  • drafting recruitment materials
  • summarising HR policies for a qualified person to check
  • translating staff guidance

docs.title: Red Uses
docs.text:
• sourcing, filtering, ranking or rejecting candidates without a governed process
  • analysing video, voice, face, emotion, personality or culture fit
  • performance monitoring or disciplinary findings
  • redundancy, pay, promotion or dismissal decisions
  • inferring health, disability, union membership or other sensitive characteristics

docs.title: Controls
docs.text:
• complete DPIA screening early
  • tell candidates or workers clearly what is used
  • test job relevance, fairness and equality impact
  • offer adjustments and a non-AI route where needed
  • define meaningful human decision and challenge
  • monitor outcomes and stop if harm appears

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• ICO: AI tools in recruitment outcomes report
  • ICO: Key data-protection considerations for AI in recruitment
  • ICO: Employee monitoring
  • EHRC: Artificial intelligence in public services

docs.title: Research and Evaluation
docs.text: AI can help search, screen, transcribe, code and summarise evidence. It can also fabricate sources, flatten minority views and make a method hard to reproduce.

docs.title: Minimum Method Record
docs.text:
Record:

  • research question and protocol
  • tool, model and date
  • data submitted and approval route
  • prompts or reproducible instructions where appropriate
  • human screening or validation sample
  • error and disagreement handling
  • citation verification against original sources
  • participant notice or consent where required
  • limitations and material AI assistance in the output

docs.title: Citation Rule
docs.text: Treat an AI citation as a lead, not proof. Open the original source and check that it exists, is authoritative, is current and supports the exact sentence.

docs.title: Do Not Use AI To
docs.text:
• invent research findings
  • create fake quotes or case studies
  • analyse raw identifiable responses in an unapproved tool
  • replace ethical review
  • conceal uncertainty

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• UKRIO: AI in research
  • UKRIO Code of Practice for Research 2025

docs.title: Safeguarding and AI
docs.text: AI must sit inside the charity's safeguarding system, not alongside it.

docs.title: July 2026 Evidence Update
docs.text:
The latest safeguarding research supports a strict boundary:

  • AI may support administrative work only where information is anonymised, approved and reviewed by trained staff.
  • AI must not replace professional judgement, safeguarding escalation, crisis support or human triage.
  • General-purpose AI should not receive safeguarding case notes, live disclosures, identifiable details about children or adults at risk, or special-category information.
  • Any beneficiary-facing AI service needs safeguarding review, data-protection review, testing, monitoring, human handoff and an incident route before launch.

docs.title: Red by Default
docs.text:
Treat these as Red for general-purpose or unapproved AI tools:

  • live safeguarding disclosures
  • case records
  • allegations or risk assessments
  • identifiable information about children or adults at risk
  • crisis, self-harm, abuse or exploitation advice
  • automated triage or downgrade of concerns

docs.title: Minimum Rules
docs.text:
• do not present AI as a counsellor, crisis worker or safeguarding lead
  • do not allow AI to close or downgrade a concern
  • make emergency and human-contact routes prominent
  • preserve records and handoffs where a disclosure occurs
  • test harmful advice, dependency, self-harm, abuse and evasion scenarios
  • let staff override or stop the service
  • involve safeguarding specialists before launch

docs.title: Ask for Specialist Review When
docs.text:
• the AI use affects a beneficiary-facing service
  • children or adults at risk may use the system
  • special category or criminal-offence data may be processed
  • a tool may make or influence a risk judgement
  • a transcript or summary may contain safeguarding information

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• Charity Commission: Safeguarding and protecting people for charities and trustees
  • Charity Commission: Charities and Artificial Intelligence

docs.title: Translation, Accessibility and Inclusion
docs.text: AI translation and accessibility tools can widen participation, but fluent output can still be wrong.

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• state the language, audience and specialist terms
  • use a qualified or community reviewer for safety-critical content
  • treat automated captions as drafts
  • preserve speaker identity and non-speech information where needed
  • offer another format or human interpreter where appropriate
  • test with assistive technology and real users where possible
  • do not infer disability or language need from unrelated behaviour
  • review synthetic voice for intelligibility, consent and impersonation risk

docs.title: Red Uses
docs.text:
• unreviewed translation of safeguarding, legal, medical or rights information
  • automated captions as the only accessible format for important content
  • synthetic voice impersonating a real person
  • AI summaries that remove essential warnings or eligibility criteria

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• W3C: WCAG overview
  • W3C: WCAG 2.2 quick reference
  • GOV.UK: Accessibility requirements for public sector websites and apps

docs.title: Volunteers
docs.text: Volunteers should receive the same practical AI safety support as staff.

docs.title: Practical Rules
docs.text:
• do not require volunteers to use personal AI accounts for charity work
  • give only the minimum data and connector access needed
  • train volunteers on Before You Paste and incident reporting
  • do not use AI alone to screen, discipline or remove a volunteer
  • separate genuine skills or availability criteria from inferred personality or fit
  • revoke AI access and connectors during offboarding
  • provide accessible, non-AI alternatives where needed

docs.title: Higher-Risk Volunteer Data
docs.text:
Pause before using AI with:

  • DBS-related or safeguarding information
  • references
  • health, disability or support needs
  • complaints or conduct issues
  • rota, attendance or performance records

docs.title: Source
docs.text: • NCVO: Volunteering strategy

docs.title: Website, Code and Analytics
docs.text: AI can help with code, content and analytics, but generated technical work should be treated as untrusted until reviewed and tested.

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• never paste secrets, tokens, private keys or real production data into an unapproved assistant
  • verify licences and provenance for generated dependencies or code
  • run tests, linting, dependency checks and peer review
  • treat generated commands and infrastructure changes as untrusted
  • avoid destructive commands unless the scope and rollback are understood
  • review analytics and personalisation for lawful basis, transparency, minimisation and PECR/cookie obligations
  • isolate experiments from production
  • preserve rollback and change records
  • require human approval for deployments and access-control changes

docs.title: Red Uses
docs.text:
• sharing live credentials
  • exposing private repository data in personal accounts
  • letting an agent deploy or change access controls without approval
  • uploading vulnerability details to an unapproved tool
  • using analytics to profile people without a lawful and transparent route

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• NCSC: AI and cyber security
  • GOV.UK: Code of Practice for the Cyber Security of AI
  • ICO AI audit framework: Information security and integrity

docs.title: Governance
docs.text:
Good AI governance should be achievable, proportionate and useful.

Trustees remain responsible for decisions made by their charity. AI can support drafting, research and analysis, but it should not be the sole basis for a material decision.

docs.title: Start With Minimum Controls
docs.text:
Small and medium charities should start with:

  • a named owner for AI use
  • simple staff rules
  • an approved-tools list
  • a basic AI-use register
  • a tool approval process
  • a supplier and connector check
  • human review expectations
  • an incident route
  • trustee visibility over high-risk use
  • a review process for changes, incidents and near misses

docs.title: Pages in This Section
docs.text:
• AI policy and risk ownership
  • Trustee briefing and assurance
  • Tool approval
  • Supplier contracts and procurement
  • Agents and connectors
  • Monitoring, audit and change control
  • AI incidents

docs.title: Trustee Questions
docs.text:
Trustees can ask:

  • What AI tools are staff already using?
  • Are personal accounts being used for charity work?
  • Which uses involve personal, confidential or safeguarding information?
  • Who approves tools and use cases?
  • What happens if AI output causes harm?
  • How are important decisions checked by humans?
  • What incidents, near misses, complaints and overrides occurred?
  • Which supplier, model, connector or terms changed since the last review?

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• Charity Commission: Charities and Artificial Intelligence
  • Charity Commission: Safeguarding and protecting people for charities and trustees
  • Fundraising Regulator: Guidance for using artificial intelligence in fundraising

docs.title: Agents and Connectors
docs.text:
Some AI tools do more than answer questions. They can search files, read email, query databases, write to systems, publish content or trigger workflows.

Treat these tools as higher risk than a standalone drafting assistant.

docs.title: Why Risk Increases
docs.text:
Risk increases when AI can:

  • access internal files or email
  • search CRM, HR, finance or case-management systems
  • retrieve confidential information the user did not intend to use
  • follow malicious instructions hidden in documents or webpages
  • send messages, edit records, publish pages or trigger payments
  • expose broadly shared files through normal user permissions

docs.title: Minimum Controls
docs.text:
• start read-only where possible
  • use least privilege
  • restrict connectors to the task
  • audit file and folder permissions before enabling AI search
  • require approval before external actions
  • block access to secrets, credentials and sensitive folders
  • log activity
  • test with failure cases
  • create a kill switch or rollback process
  • review incidents and near misses

docs.title: Map the Chain
docs.text:
Before enabling a connector or agent, map:

Layer Question Identity Which user, service account or workspace identity acts? Data source Which folders, mailboxes, databases or systems can it read? Model Which model or provider processes the data? Action Can it write, send, publish, delete, pay or change records? External recipient Could data or output leave the charity? Logs Where are prompts, outputs, actions and errors recorded?

Approve read and write scopes separately.

docs.title: Prompt Injection and Poisoned Content
docs.text:
An agent may follow instructions hidden inside emails, webpages, PDFs or shared documents. Treat untrusted content as potentially hostile.

Test whether the agent can be tricked into:

  • revealing confidential information
  • ignoring system or policy instructions
  • sending data to an external address
  • taking an action without approval
  • using a document as an instruction rather than as evidence

docs.title: Pre-Launch Test
docs.text:
Before launch:

  • test in a sandbox or low-risk workspace
  • use dummy data
  • run adversarial prompts and poisoned-document tests
  • confirm logs show what happened
  • rehearse emergency disable
  • confirm rollback for any write action
  • set transaction, sending or publishing limits

docs.title: Red Uses
docs.text:
Do not allow an agent to:

  • send external messages without approval
  • publish content without review
  • delete or change important records without approval
  • access safeguarding, HR or case records without specialist assessment
  • handle money or procurement decisions without controls
  • act on hidden instructions from untrusted content

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• NCSC: Thinking carefully before adopting agentic AI
  • NCSC: AI and cyber security
  • GOV.UK: Code of Practice for the Cyber Security of AI

docs.title: AI Policy and Risk Ownership
docs.text: Proportionate does not mean informal or undocumented. A small charity can still have clear AI rules, a named owner and an incident route.

docs.title: Minimum Policy Contents
docs.text:
An AI policy should state:

  • who owns AI governance
  • which tools, accounts and features are approved
  • what staff and volunteers must not enter
  • when to use Green, Amber and Red routes
  • when DPIA, safeguarding, HR, equality, procurement or security review is needed
  • how human review works
  • how new use cases are recorded
  • how incidents and near misses are reported
  • when trustee visibility is required

docs.title: Ownership Model
docs.text:
Assign named roles for:

Role Responsibility AI owner Maintains approved use list and review cycle Tool owner Owns a specific system, settings and users Data-protection lead Reviews personal-data risks, DPIAs and rights Safeguarding lead Reviews risks to children, adults at risk and service users IT/security lead Reviews accounts, connectors, access and incidents Trustee lead Provides board visibility and challenge

docs.title: Escalation Triggers
docs.text:
Escalate when AI may affect:

  • access to services, grants, employment or support
  • children, adults at risk or vulnerable people
  • special category or criminal-offence data
  • safeguarding or casework
  • payments, fraud checks or financial controls
  • external publication or public trust
  • connected systems or autonomous actions

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• Charity Commission: Charities and artificial intelligence
  • The essential trustee: what you need to know, what you need to do
  • NCSC: AI and cyber security
