docs.title: AI Incidents
docs.text: An AI incident is any AI-related event that may create harm, disclose information, mislead people, affect a decision unfairly or damage trust.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• personal data pasted into an unapproved tool
  • safeguarding details shared with a chatbot
  • confidential files exposed through an AI connector
  • fabricated information published as fact
  • a harmful or biased output used in a decision
  • AI-generated images presented as real evidence
  • an agent sends, deletes or changes something without approval

docs.title: First Steps
docs.text:
1. Stop the use.
  2. Preserve basic facts: tool, account, date, prompt, output and people affected.
  3. Tell the right internal lead.
  4. Do not paste more information into the tool to investigate.
  5. Follow existing data-protection, safeguarding, security or complaints processes.
  6. Do not hide the mistake, delete evidence or ask the AI tool to "clean up" the incident.

docs.title: Four Triage Lanes
docs.text:
Many AI incidents need more than one route.

Lane Examples Lead Data protection personal data pasted, transcript exposed, rights request affected Data-protection lead Safeguarding/service harmful advice, missed escalation, child or adult at risk affected Safeguarding or service lead Cyber/agent compromised account, prompt injection, connector leak, unauthorised action IT/security lead Content/reputation fabricated quote, misleading image, wrong public claim Communications or senior lead

docs.title: Review Questions
docs.text:
• Was personal or confidential information disclosed?
  • Could a child, beneficiary, staff member, volunteer or donor be harmed?
  • Was a decision affected?
  • Was the output published or shared externally?
  • Can the data be deleted or access restricted?
  • Does the charity need to notify anyone?
  • What control failed?
  • What should change before the tool is used again?

docs.title: Common Root Causes
docs.text:
The July 2026 incident research highlighted recurring causes that charities should look for during reviews:

  • lack of human oversight or qualified review
  • overreliance on AI for health, legal, safeguarding or specialist advice
  • hallucinated facts, fabricated citations or unsupported claims
  • automation bias, where people defer to the AI output without challenge
  • biased or incomplete data affecting triage, profiling or recommendations
  • connectors exposing files because source permissions were already too broad
  • unclear ownership of approval, monitoring, escalation and deletion

docs.title: Personal-Data Breach Assessment
docs.text:
Not every AI incident is a reportable personal-data breach. If a personal-data breach is likely to result in a risk to people's rights and freedoms, the charity should normally notify the ICO within 72 hours of becoming aware of it.

Record:

  • when the charity became aware
  • what data was involved
  • how many people may be affected
  • likely consequences
  • containment steps
  • whether the ICO was notified
  • whether affected people were told
  • reasons for the decision

docs.title: Other Routes to Consider
docs.text:
Depending on the facts, consider:

  • safeguarding referral or internal safeguarding review
  • Charity Commission serious-incident reporting
  • insurer notification
  • supplier or processor notice
  • police or Action Fraud route
  • payment recall or finance controls
  • contract, HR or employment process
  • public correction or takedown

docs.title: After Containment
docs.text:
• pause or narrow the affected approval
  • revoke connectors or user access if needed
  • reset credentials where relevant
  • check logs and file permissions
  • review training and policy wording
  • update the AI-use register
  • agree when the tool may be used again

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• ICO: Personal data breaches
  • ICO: 72 hours - how to respond to a personal data breach
  • Charity Commission: How to report a serious incident in your charity
  • NCSC: AI and cyber security

docs.title: Monitoring, Audit and Change Control
docs.text: AI approvals can go stale quickly. Models, features, connectors, retention settings and provider terms change.

docs.title: What to Monitor
docs.text:
• new or changed use cases
  • user access and offboarding
  • prompts, files and outputs where logs are available
  • connector permissions
  • incidents, near misses and complaints
  • human-review overrides
  • accuracy, bias or safety issues
  • provider terms, subprocessors and model changes
  • DPIA and supplier-review dates

docs.title: Change Triggers
docs.text:
Reapprove or re-screen when there is:

  • a new model
  • a new account plan
  • a new connector or agent capability
  • a new data source
  • a new purpose or affected group
  • a new external action, such as sending or publishing
  • a provider term, retention or training change
  • a material incident or near miss

docs.title: Simple Monthly Check
docs.text:
For small charities, a light monthly check can ask:

  1. Did anyone start using a new AI tool or feature?
  2. Did any approved tool change settings, terms or models?
  3. Were there any incidents, near misses or complaints?
  4. Did any user leave, change role or no longer need access?
  5. Are any Amber or Red uses missing review?

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• ICO: Accountability and governance implications of AI
  • NCSC Board Toolkit

docs.title: Supplier Contracts and Procurement
docs.text: Procure the exact AI service, plan, account, feature and configuration. A supplier's brand reputation is not enough.

docs.title: Supplier Questionnaire
docs.text:
Ask for:

  • controller, processor and subprocessor roles
  • processing purposes
  • training and product-improvement terms
  • retention, deletion and backups
  • human review by supplier staff
  • security controls and certifications
  • incident notice and support
  • subprocessors and model providers
  • international transfers
  • individual-rights support
  • connector and agent permissions
  • logging and audit controls
  • SSO, MFA and role-based access
  • accessibility and inclusion considerations
  • IP, output and content-use terms
  • change notice
  • service continuity, export and exit

docs.title: Procurement Gates
docs.text:
Require specialist review for:

  • personal, special category or criminal-offence data
  • children, adults at risk or vulnerable groups
  • write-access connectors or agents
  • credentials, secrets or security-sensitive systems
  • payment, procurement or fraud workflows
  • large file repositories or enterprise search
  • public deployment or beneficiary-facing AI
  • unclear deletion, training, subprocessor or transfer terms

docs.title: Exit Plan
docs.text:
Before approving a tool, know how the charity will:

  • remove users
  • revoke connectors
  • export required records
  • delete prompts, files and outputs where possible
  • preserve incident or audit evidence where required
  • retire links from policies, training and templates

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
• ICO AI audit framework: Contracts and third parties
  • GOV.UK: Code of Practice for the Cyber Security of AI
  • NCSC: AI and cyber security

docs.title: Tool Approval
docs.text:
Approve the tool, account plan and use case, not just the brand.

For example, a personal paid AI account is not the same as an organisational workspace with a contract, admin controls, retention settings and audit logs.

docs.title: Minimum Approval Questions
docs.text:
Ask:

  • What problem are we solving?
  • Which tool, plan and account type will be used?
  • Which model, feature, connector or agent is being approved?
  • What data will be entered or connected?
  • Will the provider use inputs or outputs for model training?
  • What retention and deletion controls exist?
  • Is there a Data Processing Agreement or suitable contract?
  • Where may data be processed or transferred?
  • What admin, SSO, MFA, audit and access controls exist?
  • Can the tool connect to email, files, CRM, HR or case systems?
  • Can the tool send, publish, delete, spend, update records or trigger workflows?
  • Who owns the use case?
  • Who reviews outputs?
  • What training is required before use?
  • What happens if the tool gives harmful or wrong output?
  • What evidence proves the settings match the approval?
  • When will the approval be reviewed?

docs.title: Minimum AI-Use Register Fields
docs.text: Field Why it matters Use ID Gives the use a stable reference Tool and plan Distinguishes brand from account controls Owner Creates accountability Purpose Reduces function creep People affected Identifies consultation and risk needs Data categories Screens personal and special category data Connectors/actions Shows expanded access Human review Names the reviewer and authority Risk rating Green, Amber or Red DPIA status Shows data-protection governance Safeguarding/equality/security screens Captures specialist review needs Training required Prevents access before users understand limits Incident contact Gives users a route when something goes wrong Approval and review date Supports lifecycle control Change triggers Defines when approval expires

docs.title: Approval Outcomes
docs.text:
• Approved: use may proceed within the agreed scope.
  • Approved with limits: use may proceed only with specific controls.
  • Needs specialist review: data protection, safeguarding, legal, HR or procurement review is needed.
  • Not approved: do not use for charity work.
  • Retired: use has ended; users, connectors and stored data have been reviewed.

docs.title: Example Approval Wording
docs.text:
Good approval:

Tool: ChatGPT Business workspace. Feature: drafting only, no connectors. Purpose: first-draft public campaign copy from public information. Data: no personal or confidential data. Users: communications team after training. Reviewer: Communications Manager. Review date: October 2026.

Bad approval:

ChatGPT is approved.

docs.title: Common Change Triggers
docs.text:
Reapprove when there is:

  • a new model
  • a new connector
  • a new data category
  • a new purpose
  • a new affected group
  • a new external action
  • a new supplier term, retention setting or subprocessor
  • an incident, complaint or near miss

docs.title: Trustee Briefing and Assurance
docs.text: Trustees remain responsible for charity decisions involving AI. AI can inform decisions, but it cannot carry trustee judgement or accountability.

docs.title: Board Questions
docs.text:
Ask:

  • What AI tools are staff, volunteers and contractors using?
  • Which uses involve personal, confidential, safeguarding or financial information?
  • Which uses could affect access to services, employment, grants or support?
  • Who approves tools, account plans, connectors and use cases?
  • How are outputs checked by competent people?
  • What incidents, near misses, complaints and overrides occurred?
  • Which supplier, model, connector or terms changed?
  • Are any approvals, DPIAs or reviews overdue?
  • What decisions need trustee input?

docs.title: Trustee Dashboard
docs.text:
A simple assurance report can include:

  • approved tools and use cases
  • high-risk proposals under review
  • new models, connectors or agents
  • incidents and near misses
  • training completion
  • DPIA and supplier-review status
  • user access and offboarding checks
  • planned retirements or changes

docs.title: July 2026 Assurance Measures
docs.text:
Trustees should be able to see, at least quarterly for larger or higher-risk organisations:

Measure What trustees should ask AI policy Is there a board-approved policy covering approved uses, prohibited uses, data, safeguarding, procurement and incidents? AI use register What tools and use cases exist, who owns them and when were they reviewed? DPIAs and risk assessments Which AI uses involve personal data, profiling, connectors, beneficiaries, children or adults at risk? Training Have staff, volunteers and trustees been trained on the charity's AI rules? Incidents and near misses What went wrong, what was learned and what changed? Connectors Which tools can search internal files, email, CRM or case systems, and how are permissions controlled?

docs.title: When Trustees Should Be Involved
docs.text:
Bring a use to trustees when it may materially affect:

  • strategy, reputation or public trust
  • safeguarding or vulnerable people
  • major service delivery
  • employment or volunteer rights
  • fundraising ethics
  • material contracts or spend
  • serious incidents

docs.title: Privacy
docs.text: This page describes the current public website at a high level. It should be reviewed whenever the site adds analytics, forms, newsletter signup, embedded media, comments, search telemetry, a chatbot or any other feature that collects personal data.

docs.title: Current Site Position
docs.text:
As currently configured in this repository, the public site:

  • is a static MkDocs site hosted on GitHub Pages
  • does not include a contact form
  • does not include analytics configured by this project
  • does not include a newsletter signup
  • does not include a chatbot
  • does not intentionally collect personal data from visitors

GitHub and browsers may process technical information needed to serve the site. Review GitHub's own privacy information for platform-level processing.

docs.title: What Must Not Be Submitted Publicly
docs.text: Do not submit personal data, safeguarding information, identifiable beneficiary stories, employee records, donor records, passwords, API keys, confidential charity information or raw research responses through public GitHub issues or public project spaces.

docs.title: Future Data Inventory
docs.text:
Before adding any interactive feature, complete a data inventory covering:

  • controller identity and contact route
  • hosting and security logs
  • analytics and cookies
  • contact forms or email
  • mailing lists
  • embedded media
  • GitHub contribution workflow
  • processors and international transfers
  • retention
  • individual rights and complaint route
  • whether children may use the service
  • whether any automated decision-making is involved

docs.title: Principle
docs.text: The project should minimise personal data collection and keep research inputs separate from public guidance.

docs.title: Quick Check
docs.text:
Five questions before using AI

1. Is this exact tool, account and feature approved for charity work? Yes, for this task Not sure No 2. What will you enter, upload, record or connect? Public, fictional or synthetic information Internal but not personal information Personal data about donors, staff, volunteers or service users Health, disability, HR, criminal-offence or other sensitive data Safeguarding, casework, children or adults at risk Passwords, API keys or security-sensitive information 3. What will the AI do? Draft, rewrite or summarise Search connected files, email, CRM or case systems Score, rank, profile or recommend something about people Send, publish, delete, spend money or change records 4. Could the output affect services, money, jobs, safeguarding or a public claim? No Maybe Yes 5. Will a named person check the output before it is used? Yes Not sure No Clear answers

Not enough information yet

Answer each question to get a route

The check will show a Green, Amber or Red route and link to the next page.

docs.title: Quick Check
docs.text: Answer these questions to get a practical Green, Amber or Red route. This is a triage aid, not a substitute for your charity's policy, DPIA, safeguarding or legal process.

docs.title: If You Are Already Worried
docs.text: If you have already pasted, uploaded, connected, recorded or published something risky, stop using the tool for that task and follow the AI incident route.

docs.title: Research and Community
docs.text:
This section invites research, feedback and practical collaboration on safer AI use in charities.

AI Safe Charity Index was originally created by Matthew Nel as an open, research-led resource for UK charities.

Request Collaborator Access

docs.title: July 2026 Research Additions
docs.text:
Evidence snapshot Twenty 2026 findings A public synthesis of adoption, governance, public trust, safeguarding, tool controls and connector risks.

Sources Source library Curated links to regulators, sector research, safeguarding evidence and official vendor documentation.

Contributors Evidence system Evidence levels, contribution rules, source submission checks and review expectations.

Research agenda Gaps and priorities Priority questions for charity AI governance, beneficiary views, incidents, connectors and public trust.

docs.title: Research Interests
docs.text:
The project welcomes research conversations on:

  • AI policy in charities
  • responsible AI governance for small and medium charities
  • trustee oversight and assurance
  • staff confidence, training and adoption barriers
  • AI use in fundraising, communications, HR, governance and service delivery
  • AI, safeguarding and vulnerable people
  • AI, GDPR and data minimisation in charity workflows

docs.title: Ways to Contribute
docs.text:
You can help by:

  • suggesting sources
  • reviewing draft guidance
  • proposing practical examples
  • sharing anonymised workflow insights
  • testing pages for clarity and accessibility
  • identifying missing topics
  • contributing to research packs

Do not submit personal data, safeguarding case details, confidential charity material or private research responses through public project spaces.

docs.title: Becoming a Collaborator
docs.text:
If you want to contribute regularly, request a GitHub collaborator invite from Matthew on LinkedIn.

Include:

  • your GitHub username
  • the contribution area you want to help with
  • relevant charity, research, policy, safeguarding, GDPR, cyber-security, accessibility or UX experience
  • whether you want to contribute sources, review content, improve templates, test the site or help with design

Suggested subject line:

AI Safe Charity Index collaborator invite request

Request via LinkedIn

Do not include personal data, safeguarding case details, confidential charity information or private research responses in the first message. If a conversation needs to move to email, Matthew can share an email address privately.

Once approved, Matthew can invite you through GitHub. You will need to accept the GitHub invitation before you can collaborate directly on the project.

docs.title: 2026 Evidence Snapshot
docs.text: This page distils the July 2026 research packs into public-facing findings for UK charities. It is a synthesis, not a replacement for legal, safeguarding, data-protection or procurement advice.

docs.title: What Changed
docs.text:
Adoption AI is now ordinary workplace behaviour Sector surveys indicate widespread charity use of AI, especially for drafting, bids, communications, research and administration.

Governance Oversight is lagging behind use Many charities still lack a clear AI policy, use register, approval process, trustee dashboard or incident route.

Trust Public acceptance is conditional People are more comfortable with back-office AI than with AI influencing support, eligibility, safeguarding or sensitive personal data.

docs.title: Adoption and Governance
docs.text:
1. AI use is now mainstream across UK charities, with reported use concentrated in drafting, research, fundraising, administration and content work.
  2. Governance has not kept pace. The priority is not simply "more AI", but safer AI: policies, registers, training, DPIAs, supplier checks and trustee oversight.
  3. Trustees may underestimate actual AI use because staff and volunteers often experiment through consumer accounts before formal approval.
  4. Shadow AI is a major risk route. Consumer tools can bypass organisational contracts, retention settings, training controls and audit logs.

docs.title: Public Trust and Beneficiaries
docs.text:
1. Public attitudes are cautious and conditional. Trust falls when AI influences decisions about who receives help or when sensitive personal data is involved.
  2. Charities should be transparent when AI materially shapes public content, donor communications or service journeys.
  3. General-purpose chatbots are unsafe substitutes for crisis support, safeguarding, clinical, legal or benefits advice.
  4. Synthetic images and synthetic beneficiary stories can damage trust if audiences may confuse them with real people or real events.

docs.title: Risk Patterns
docs.text:
1. Hallucinated facts, fabricated citations and confident misinformation remain core failure modes.
  2. Health, mental-health, safeguarding and legal contexts require human professional judgement and verified sources.
  3. Bias and discrimination risks are well documented in public-sector algorithmic decision-making and are directly relevant to charity profiling, triage and eligibility.
  4. Charity-specific AI incident evidence is still sparse, so near-miss learning should be deliberately collected.

docs.title: Regulation and Oversight
docs.text:
1. UK GDPR, the Data Protection Act 2018 and PECR apply to AI-enabled charity activities where relevant.
  2. Charity Commission expectations around trustee duties still apply when AI is used.
  3. The Fundraising Regulator expects charitable fundraising institutions to remain responsible and accountable for AI-generated outputs they use.
  4. AI governance should sit inside existing risk, safeguarding, data-protection, procurement and incident processes.

docs.title: Tools and Connectors
docs.text:
1. Enterprise AI tools can offer stronger controls than consumer accounts, but no plan is automatically safe for every charity use.
  2. Connectors to SharePoint, Drive, Slack, email or CRMs are a critical risk point because they can expose existing permission problems at scale.
  3. "Not used for training" is useful but incomplete. Charities still need to check retention, deletion, subprocessors, human access, audit logs, DPA terms and connector scope.
  4. The practical route is proportionate: Green uses with normal review, Amber uses with documented checks, and Red uses redesigned or stopped.

docs.title: Implications for This Site
docs.text:
The July 2026 research strengthens the site's existing direction:

  • keep the Before You Paste rule simple and prominent
  • treat beneficiary-facing AI, donor profiling, recruitment scoring and autonomous agents as high-risk
  • make account-plan and connector checks part of tool approval
  • give trustees a one-page assurance view
  • invite contributors to improve the evidence base with clear source standards

docs.title: Contributor and Evidence System
docs.text: AI Safe Charity Index should stay useful because it is practical, but trustworthy because it is research-led. This page sets the contribution rules for research updates, source suggestions and page reviews.

docs.title: Evidence Levels
docs.text: Level Evidence type How to use it A Legislation and statutory regulators Highest authority for legal and regulatory duties. B Official government and public-body guidance Strong guidance, especially where charities operate in comparable contexts. C Peer-reviewed academic research Strong for harms, bias, evaluation and methods; may need translation into charity practice. D Established charity-sector research Useful for adoption, practice and sector context; check methodology and sponsorship. E Official vendor documentation Necessary for product features, DPAs, retention and training settings, but not independent proof of safety. F Credible professional analysis Useful interpretation; verify against higher-level sources where possible. G Documented organisational examples Useful examples; may not generalise across charity sizes or service types. H Reputable media reporting Useful for incidents and public debate; verify with primary sources where possible. I Anecdotal or unverified claims Do not use for substantive claims without corroboration.

docs.title: Contribution Rules
docs.text:
• Separate facts, interpretation and recommendations.
  • Do not add vendor marketing claims without independent evidence.
  • Do not submit personal data, safeguarding case details or confidential charity material.
  • Use direct source links, not search-result pages.
  • Record source limitations, especially sponsorship, small sample size or non-UK context.
  • Flag where legal, safeguarding, cyber-security, employment or data-protection review is needed.
  • Keep public pages plain-English and usable by small charities.

docs.title: Source Submission Checklist
docs.text:
For each source, capture:

  • organisation or author
  • title
  • date and last updated date
  • jurisdiction
  • source type and evidence level
  • direct URL
  • date accessed
  • relevance to charity AI safety
  • limitations or conflicts of interest
  • pages or claims the source supports

docs.title: Page Review Checklist
docs.text:
Before a page is treated as stable, check:

  • Are high-impact claims sourced?
  • Are Green, Amber and Red recommendations proportionate?
  • Is the page clear about when specialist advice is needed?
  • Does it avoid giving legal, safeguarding or clinical advice?
  • Does it explain uncertainty rather than hiding it?
  • Are links current and direct?
  • Is the language plain enough for busy staff and trustees?

docs.title: Correction and Challenge Process
docs.text:
If you think a page is wrong or incomplete:

  1. Identify the page and section.
  2. Explain the issue.
  3. Provide evidence or a source link.
  4. Suggest a correction.
  5. Declare any relevant conflict of interest.

For regular contribution, request collaborator access through LinkedIn.

docs.title: Research Gaps and Priorities
docs.text: The evidence base for charity AI use is growing quickly, but important questions remain unanswered. This page turns the July 2026 research packs into a practical agenda for researchers, funders and collaborators.

docs.title: Priority Gaps
docs.text: Gap Why it matters Suggested method Priority Charity-size-specific adoption and governance Micro, small, medium and large charities need different controls and support. Stratified sector survey plus interviews by income band and activity type. High, 6-12 months Beneficiaries' views, especially children and adults at risk AI governance must reflect the people affected by charity services, not only staff views. Participatory research, focus groups and interviews with strong ethical safeguards. High, 6-12 months Charity-specific AI incidents and near misses Charities need real lessons about data leaks, unsafe advice, bias, profiling and reputational harm. Anonymous reporting pilot and case-study collection. High, 6-12 months Independent testing of workplace AI controls and connectors Vendor documentation is necessary but not independent evidence of safety. Controlled charity-environment testing of permissions, retention, connectors and oversharing. High, 12 months Volunteer and shadow AI use Informal use may be where the most sensitive mistakes happen. Anonymous surveys and focus groups with staff and volunteers. High, 6-12 months Effectiveness of governance interventions Policies and registers should be tested for whether they actually reduce risk. Action research with pre/post measures in partner charities. High, 12 months Long-term organisational effects AI may change accountability, staff judgement and organisational culture over time. Longitudinal case studies. Medium, 12+ months Public trust and synthetic media AI-generated imagery and composite stories can affect trust and fundraising ethics. Public surveys and controlled campaign testing with ethical safeguards. Medium, 12 months Accessibility and digital poverty AI can assist inclusion or deepen exclusion. Research with people facing digital poverty, accessibility barriers or language barriers. Medium, 12+ months Environmental impact AI use has sustainability implications that are rarely addressed in charity governance. Scoping study and practical reporting guidance. Low-medium, 12+ months

docs.title: Six-Month Research Agenda
docs.text:
1. Size-specific adoption and governance survey.
  2. Volunteer and shadow AI use study.
  3. Beneficiary perception scoping study.
  4. Anonymous incident and near-miss reporting pilot.
  5. Public-trust synthesis focused on AI in charity decisions and synthetic media.

docs.title: Twelve-Month Research Agenda
docs.text:
1. Independent testing of workplace AI connectors and controls.
  2. Longitudinal organisational impact case studies.
  3. Synthetic media and fundraising trust experiments.
  4. Accessibility and digital poverty study.
  5. Governance intervention evaluation projects.
  6. Environmental impact scoping.

docs.title: Invitation to Researchers
docs.text: This project welcomes collaboration on charity AI policy, safeguarding, public trust, donor ethics, data protection, procurement and practical governance. If you want to contribute research, start with the evidence system and request collaborator access through LinkedIn.

Approved tool does not mean approved use

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
This section covers tool evaluation and guidance for common AI products.

Tool guidance must be checked against current provider documentation before procurement or rollout. Product terms, retention settings, training settings and admin controls change frequently.

docs.title: Core Rule
docs.text:
Do not approve a brand name alone. Approve the tool, account plan, settings and use case.

For example, a personal paid account may not provide the same protections as a business or enterprise workspace.

docs.title: Tool Pages
docs.text:
• Account plans and data controls
  • ChatGPT
  • Claude
  • Google Gemini
  • Microsoft Copilot
  • Notion AI
  • Perplexity
  • Canva AI
  • Adobe Firefly

docs.title: Every Tool Review Checks
docs.text:
• what the tool does
  • plan and account differences
  • whether inputs may be used for model training
  • retention and deletion controls
  • admin controls
  • connectors and actions
  • suitable charity uses
  • Green, Amber and Red use cases
  • human review requirements
  • known limitations

docs.title: Cross-Tool Approval Matrix
docs.text: Check Why it matters Brand Names the service, but does not prove suitability Account plan Determines contract, admin controls and support route Model or feature Different features can have different data flows Training setting Important, but only one part of the risk Retention and deletion Shows how long prompts, files and outputs remain Connectors Expands the tool's access beyond the chat window Actions Shows whether the AI can send, publish, delete, spend or update Data ceiling Defines the most sensitive information allowed Human review Keeps accountability with a competent person Review trigger Prevents stale approvals after provider changes

docs.title: Claims That Do Not Prove Safe Use
docs.text:
• "It is paid."
  • "It says enterprise."
  • "It does not train on our data."
  • "It has citations."
  • "The user already has access to the files."
  • "The supplier is well known."
  • "We can delete the chat."
  • "Other charities use it."

These claims may be relevant, but none of them replaces tool, plan, feature, data, purpose, contract and human-review checks.

docs.title: Account Plans and Data Controls
docs.text:
The tool brand alone does not tell you whether a charity use is appropriate.

Different account types can have different:

  • contracts
  • Data Processing Agreements
  • model-training settings
  • retention settings
  • admin controls
  • audit logs
  • connectors
  • deletion processes
  • support arrangements

docs.title: Personal Accounts
docs.text: Use personal accounts only for public, non-confidential Green tasks unless the charity has explicitly assessed and approved the use.

docs.title: Organisational Accounts
docs.text:
Business, team, education, charity or enterprise plans may provide stronger controls, but they still need a use-case assessment.

Check:

  • current official documentation
  • contract and DPA
  • admin settings
  • training and feedback settings
  • retention and deletion
  • connectors and third-party apps
  • audit access
  • who owns the workspace

docs.title: Do Not Assume
docs.text:
• paid means approved
  • no training means no retention
  • enterprise means every use is safe
  • user deletion means immediate back-end deletion
  • citations mean a source has been checked
  • connectors are safe because the user already had access

docs.title: "Not Used for Training" Is Not Enough
docs.text:
A no-training commitment is useful, but it answers only one question. You still need to check:

  • retention
  • human access by the provider
  • subprocessors and model providers
  • security controls
  • transfers
  • deletion and backups
  • connectors
  • output risk
  • lawful purpose
  • charity-controlled settings

docs.title: July 2026 Tool-Control Findings
docs.text:
The latest tool-control matrix found a consistent pattern across major workplace AI products:

  • business and enterprise plans usually have stronger controls than consumer accounts
  • consumer accounts remain unsuitable for charity work involving personal, confidential, safeguarding, HR, donor or case information
  • retention, deletion, audit logs, SSO, SCIM, DPA terms and subprocessors vary by vendor and plan
  • connector safety depends heavily on the underlying permissions in SharePoint, OneDrive, Google Drive, Slack, Notion, email and CRM systems
  • charity discounts or nonprofit access may exist, but discounted access does not remove the need for risk assessment

Treat official vendor documentation as necessary evidence for features and terms, not as independent proof that a tool is safe for a particular charity use.

docs.title: Minimum Account Evidence
docs.text:
Keep a dated record of:

  • official provider documentation checked
  • account plan and workspace name
  • contract or DPA location
  • admin settings screenshots or exports
  • allowed data categories
  • disabled features or connectors
  • user groups allowed
  • review date and change triggers

docs.title: Practical Approval Rule
docs.text:
Write approvals like this:

Tool: ChatGPT Business. Use case: drafting public campaign copy from public information. Data: no personal or confidential data. Reviewer: Communications Manager. Review date: October 2026.

Avoid approvals like this:

ChatGPT is approved.

docs.title: Adobe Firefly
docs.text: Adobe Firefly can generate and edit images, vectors, video, audio and design assets.

docs.title: Green Uses
docs.text:
• generic illustrations and textures
  • background extension or removal on assets the charity owns
  • concept development
  • non-personal campaign assets reviewed by a designer

docs.title: Amber Uses
docs.text:
• synthetic people or emotionally sensitive scenes
  • editing images of real beneficiaries
  • third-party or partner models
  • campaign assets where rights, publicity or trademark issues matter

docs.title: Red Uses
docs.text:
• false documentary imagery presented as a real event
  • changing safeguarding or incident evidence
  • uploading material without rights
  • deepfake audio or video of a real person

docs.title: July 2026 Research Update - 2026-07-12
docs.text:
Added draft pages for DPIA triage, automated decisions, AI fundraising, meeting transcription, tool approval, agents/connectors, AI incidents and common AI tool reviews.

Updated Before You Paste, AI and Data Protection, Governance, Tools, FAQ and Glossary using the July 2026 research batch.

docs.title: July 2026 Page Population Update - 2026-07-12
docs.text:
Added everyday-work guides for communications, beneficiary services, safeguarding, recruitment and HR, volunteers, finance and fraud, research and evaluation, translation and inclusion, and website/code/analytics.

Added data-protection lifecycle pages for lawful basis and transparency, special category data and vulnerable groups, individual rights, suppliers and transfers, and retention/security/breaches.

Added governance pages for AI policy ownership, trustee assurance, supplier procurement, and monitoring/change control. Expanded incidents, tool approvals, agents/connectors, account-plan checks, FAQ and glossary.

Known limitations:

  • automated-decision guidance remains interim pending final ICO guidance
  • high-risk GDPR, safeguarding, HR and fundraising scenarios require human or specialist review
  • provider claims must be rechecked against current official documentation before procurement
  • new page-population content is draft pending legal, safeguarding, employment/equality and security review
